aliases: Apple cider vinegar with the mother

english: Apple cider vinegar with the mother

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers vinegar broadly; apple cider vinegar is a specific fermented apple-derived vinegar subtype within this category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of apple cider vinegar is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15 g), commonly diluted in water or used as a salad dressing component.

nutrient_source.title: Apple cider vinegar — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for apple cider vinegar (cr_id F10030003). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cider vinegar (FDC ID 173469) for secondary validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: Vinegar and acetic acid: glycemic index and postprandial glucose response — peer-reviewed evidence
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Peer-reviewed literature (Johnston CS et al., Diabetes Care 2004; Liljeberg H & Björck I, Eur J Clin Nutr 1998)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Apple cider vinegar has a very low carbohydrate content (0.93 g/100 g). A GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed evidence that acetic acid in vinegar significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response; the University of Sydney GI Database lists vinegar-type condiments in the low-GI range. Glycemic load per serving = (36 × 0.93 × 15/100) / 100 = 0.05, rounded to 0.06. The practical glycemic impact is negligible at typical serving sizes.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (73 mg/100 g); acetic acid may support modest vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Apple cider vinegar is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (5 mg/100 g) and potassium contribution (73 mg/100 g); use 1–2 tablespoons per day in diluted form, and do not substitute for prescribed antihypertensive medication.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.93 g/100 g) and acetic acid-mediated reduction of postprandial glucose via delayed gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Apple cider vinegar is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its negligible carbohydrate load and evidence-based acetic acid mechanism for blunting postprandial glucose response; monitor blood glucose closely if using alongside insulin or sulfonylureas as hypoglycaemia potentiation is possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low sodium, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and trace potassium and iron content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Apple cider vinegar poses minimal cardiovascular risk due to its negligible sodium (5 mg/100 g), zero saturated fat, and zero cholesterol; it may be used freely as a condiment in heart-healthy dietary patterns, but should be diluted to protect oesophageal and dental enamel integrity.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenols and acetic acid in unfiltered varieties may support gut microbiome health relevant to immune modulation in RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Apple cider vinegar, particularly unfiltered varieties containing the mother culture, may offer modest anti-inflammatory support via polyphenols and gut microbiome modulation; however, it should not replace disease-modifying therapy and excessive quantities should be avoided due to gastrointestinal acidity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple cider vinegar (ACV) is a fermented condiment produced through a two-stage fermentation process: apple juice is first converted to hard cider by yeast (alcoholic fermentation), then acetic acid bacteria convert the alcohol to acetic acid (acetification). The result is an amber-coloured, thin-textured liquid with a sharp, tart, acidic flavour and a characteristic fruity undertone. It is widely used as a salad dressing base, pickling agent, marinade component, and health tonic. Nutritionally, ACV is very low in calories (21 kcal/100 g), contains negligible protein (0 g) and fat (0 g), and has a minimal carbohydrate content of 0.93 g/100 g with 0.4 g sugar. It provides small amounts of potassium (73 mg/100 g), calcium (7 mg/100 g), and iron (0.2 mg/100 g), and is very low in sodium (5 mg/100 g). The primary bioactive compound is acetic acid, which has been studied for its ability to reduce postprandial blood glucose by slowing gastric emptying, making ACV of clinical interest for Type 2 Diabetes management. Its very low sodium content makes it suitable for hypertension-conscious diets. Unfiltered, unpasteurised varieties containing the mother culture (a colony of acetic acid bacteria and cellulose) are associated with additional polyphenol content and potential probiotic-adjacent gut health benefits relevant to inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. ACV is suitable for vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free dietary patterns. It should always be diluted before consumption to protect tooth enamel and the oesophageal lining.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F10030003, confidence 0.95), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cider vinegar. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature given the negligible carbohydrate content. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a fermented apple-derived vinegar.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: apple cider vinegar is a fermented liquid condiment with no protein-bearing macronutrients; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173469). fat_g is naturally zero: apple cider vinegar contains no fat by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero as this is a fat-free fermented liquid. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: the fermentation and filtration process removes all fibre from the apple substrate. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero: plant-derived fermented product contains no cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for apple cider vinegar are not consistently reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the level of individual vitamins beyond those listed; values are not available from approved sources as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 36 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed evidence (Johnston CS et al., Diabetes Care 2004; Liljeberg H & Björck I, Eur J Clin Nutr 1998) and University of Sydney GI Database classification of vinegar-type condiments as low-GI; the practical glycemic load at a 15 g serving is negligible (approximately 0.06). The input record listed a rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry with suitable: false but a rationale describing anti-inflammatory properties; this has been corrected to reflect the nuanced clinical picture — ACV is not contraindicated in RA but is not a primary therapeutic agent. Food is not cuisine-specific (Western/global condiment); no mandatory multilingual local-language names apply beyond English.
